flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of heap file organization?
flashcards.back: Constant time insertion as new records are added at end of file without sorting.

flashcards.front: How do sorted files improve search performance?
flashcards.back: Binary search enables O(log n) access for equality searches on sort key.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of an index structure?
flashcards.back: Provides efficient lookup mechanism without scanning entire data file.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes clustered vs non-clustered indexes?
flashcards.back: Clustered indexes determine physical order of data records, non-clustered maintain separate structure.

flashcards.front: Why are B+ trees preferred over B-trees for databases?
flashcards.back: B+ trees store data only in leaf nodes, allowing more keys per node and better sequential access.

flashcards.front: What is the main drawback of static hashing?
flashcards.back: Fixed hash function leads to performance degradation as data grows beyond initial buckets.
Creates hierarchy of indexes where upper levels point to lower-level index blocks.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of a sparse index?
flashcards.back: Contains index entries only for some search key values, reducing storage overhead.

flashcards.front: What makes ISAM (Indexed Sequential Access Method) vulnerable?
flashcards.back: Overflow chains degrade performance as inserts accumulate in overflow areas.

flashcards.front: Why are bitmap indexes effective for low-cardinality data?
flashcards.back: They use bit arrays to efficiently represent multiple possible values in columns.

flashcards.front: What is the key advantage of extendible hashing?
flashcards.back: Dynamic expansion of hash table without complete reorganization.

flashcards.front: How does a covering index optimize queries?
flashcards.back: Contains all fields required by query, eliminating need to access data file.
